name: Preserved Duck Eggs (Thousand Year Old Eggs)
recipeIngredient: 1 cup lime (available in garden stores and nurseries)
recipeInstructions:
1. Combine the tea, salt, pine wood ashes, charcoal ashes, and fireplace ashes in a large bowl.
2. Mix well to ensure an even distribution of the ashes.
3. Using about 1/2 cup per egg, thoroughly coat each duck egg completely with this clay-like mixture.
4. Line a large crock (large ceramic or stone container) with garden soil.
5. Carefully place the coated eggs on top of the soil in the crock.
6. Cover the eggs with more soil and place the crock in a cool, dark place.
7. Allow the eggs to cure for 100 days.
8. To remove the coating, gently scrape off the eggshell surface using a soft brush or cloth.
9. Rinse the eggs under running water until clean.
10. Crack the eggs lightly and carefully peel away the shells.
11. The white of the egg will appear grayish, translucent, and have a gelatinous texture. The yolk, when sliced, will be grayish-green in color.
To serve:
- Cut the preserved duck eggs into wedges.
- Serve with sweet pickled scallions or any other sweet pickled vegetable.
- Accompany with a dipping sauce made from equal parts vinegar, soy sauce, and rice wine, mixed with minced ginger root.
